{{Short description|American psychologist (born 1938)}}
{{Infobox person
| name          = Daryl J. Bem
| image         = DarylBem (cropped).jpg
| image_caption = Bem at a conference in 1983.
| birth_date    = {{nowrap|{{Birth date and age|1938|6|10}}}}
| death_date    =
|alma_mater=[[University of Michigan]]
| occupation    = [[Social psychology|Social psychologist]], [[Parapsychology|parapsychologist]]
| known_for     = [[Self-perception theory]]
| website       = [http://dbem.org/ dbem.org]
}}

'''Daryl J. Bem''' (born June 10, 1938) is a [[social psychology|social psychologist]] and professor [[emeritus]] at [[Cornell University]]. He is the originator of the [[self-perception theory]] of [[Attitude (psychology)|attitude]] formation and change. He has also researched [[Psi (parapsychology)|psi]] phenomena,<ref name="BlackmoreSI2019">{{cite journal |last1=Blackmore |first1=Susan |title=Another scandal for psychology: Daryl Bem's data massage |journal=Skeptical Inquirer |date=2019 |volume=43 |issue=6 |page=5}}</ref><ref>Chandler, David L. (February 15, 1993). Study finds evidence of ESP phenomenon. ''[[Boston Globe]]''</ref> group [[decision making]], [[handwriting analysis]], [[sexual orientation]], and [[personality psychology|personality]] theory and assessment.

== Early life and education ==
{{BLP self-published|section|https://dbem.org/bem_bio.htm|date=January 2022}}
Bem received a [[Bachelor of Arts|BA]] in [[physics]] from [[Reed College]] in [[Portland, Oregon]], in 1960 and began graduate work in physics at the [[Massachusetts Institute of Technology]]. The [[civil rights movement]] had just begun, and he became so intrigued with the changing attitudes toward desegregation in the American South that he decided to switch fields and pursue a career as a social psychologist specializing in attitudes and public opinion. He obtained his [[Doctor of Philosophy|PhD]] in social psychology from the [[University of Michigan]] in 1964.<ref name=bem_bio/>

== Career ==

Bem taught at [[Carnegie Mellon University]], Stanford, Harvard, and Cornell University. He started at Cornell in 1978 and retired in 2007,<ref name="bem_bio">{{cite web|title=Daryl Bem: Brief Biography|url=https://dbem.org/bem_bio.htm|url-status=live|access-date=2022-01-15|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140925082732/http://dbem.org/bem_bio.htm |archive-date=2014-09-25 }}</ref> becoming a professor emeritus.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://dbem.ws/ |title=Daryl Bem's home page |date=September 9, 2010 |work=dbem.ws |access-date=January 8, 2011}}</ref>

Bem testified before a subcommittee of the [[United States Senate]] on the psychological effects of [[police]] [[interrogation]]<ref>Staff report (July 21, 1966). Senators Hear an Interrogation In Study of Confession Ruling ''[[The New York Times]]''</ref> and served as an expert witness in court cases involving sex discrimination.<ref name=bem_bio/>

=== Self-perception theory ===
In 1972, Bem proposed the [[self-perception theory]] of attitude change,<ref name="Myers146">Myers, p. 146.</ref> which proposes a different mechanism of change than that of [[Leon Festinger]]'s [[cognitive dissonance theory]]. The two theories appear contradictory. Dissonance theory explains how people change their attitudes when they find themselves acting in opposition to the attitudes they already hold, while self-perception theory explains how people create their attitudes in the first place.<ref name="Myers151">Myers, p. 151.</ref>

According to self-perception theory, people infer their attitudes from their own behavior much as an outside observer might.<ref name="Myers146"/> For example, just as a person might watch someone else giving a pro-Fidel Castro speech and infer that the person is in favor of Castro, a person who is asked to give such a speech would consequently come to view themself as more in favor of Castro.

In 1970, Bem and Keith McConnell demonstrated that people are often unaware of attitude changes caused by their behavior.  Instead, people believe that the views they currently hold are the views that they have always held.  Bem and McConnell demonstrated this by measuring the change in Carnegie Mellon students' attitudes toward having control of their university curriculum. After an initial measurement of student attitudes, which were favorable toward student control, Bem and McConnel assigned students to write an essay opposing student control. By writing an essay proclaiming views different from the ones they held, students' views changed to become less favorable toward student control. When Bem and McConnell asked the students how they had felt at the beginning of the study, students insisted that they had always felt less favorable toward student control.  The students believed that the experiment had not changed their opinions, even though it had changed their opinions significantly.<ref>{{cite book|last=Myers|first=David G.|title=Social Psychology|url=https://archive.org/details/socialpsychology00myer_173|url-access=limited|year=2010|publisher=McGraw Hill|isbn=978-0-07-337066-8|page=[https://archive.org/details/socialpsychology00myer_173/page/n110 86]|edition=Tenth}}</ref>

=== Exotic becomes erotic theory ===
Bem's [[exotic becomes erotic theory]] (EBE) presents one possible explanation as to what differentiates the [[etiology]] of [[homosexuality]] from [[heterosexuality]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Bem|first=Daryl J.|title=Exotic becomes erotic: A developmental theory of sexual orientation.|journal=Psychological Review|date=January 1996|volume=103|issue=2|pages=320–335|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.103.2.320|url=http://dbem.ws/Exotic%20Becomes%20Erotic.pdf|access-date=2009-02-28|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070411083103/http://www.dbem.ws/Exotic%20Becomes%20Erotic.pdf|archive-date=2007-04-11}}</ref> Bem theorized that the influence of biological factors on sexual orientation may be mediated by experiences in childhood, that the child's temperament predisposes the child to prefer certain activities over others.<ref>{{cite book|editor-last=Bancroft|editor-first=John |title=The role of theory in sex research|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BzKwnv0D0qIC&pg=PA67|year=2000|publisher=Indiana University Press|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-0-253-33706-1|pages=67–81}}</ref> Bem noted that because of their temperament, which is influenced by biological variables such as genetic factors, some children will be attracted to activities that are commonly enjoyed by other children of the same gender, while others will prefer activities that are typical of the other gender. Bem theorized that this makes a gender-conforming child feel different from opposite-gender children, while gender-nonconforming children will feel different from children of their own gender. He believes that this feeling of difference evokes physiological arousal when the child is near members of the gender which the child considers as being "different". Bem theorizes that this physiological arousal is later transformed into sexual arousal: that is, as adults, people become sexually attracted to the gender which they came to see as different, or "exotic", while they were children.<ref>{{cite news|last=Simpson|first=Kevin|author2=Gorski, Eric|title=Pastor's case stirs debate|url=http://www.denverpost.com/news/ci_4644918|access-date=February 1, 2012|newspaper=Denver Post|date=November 12, 2006}}</ref>

Bem based this theory in part on the finding that a majority of [[gay men]] and lesbians report being gender-nonconforming during their childhood years. A meta-analysis of 48 studies showed [[childhood gender nonconformity]] to be the strongest predictor of a homosexual orientation for both men and women.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Bailey|first=J. M.|author2=Zucker, K. J.|title=Childhood sex-typed behavior and sexual orientation: A conceptual analysis and quantitative review.|journal=Developmental Psychology|date=January 1995|volume=31|issue=1|pages=43–55|doi=10.1037/0012-1649.31.1.43|s2cid=28174284 |url=http://www.lscp.net/persons/ramus/fr/GDP1/papers/bailey95.pdf}}{{Dead link|date=July 2019 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> Bem also noted that in a study by the Kinsey Institute of approximately 1000 gay men and lesbians (and a control group of 500 heterosexual men and women), 63% of both gay men and lesbians reported that they did not like activities typical of their sex in childhood, compared with only 10–15% of heterosexual men and women. Bem also drew from six prospective studies, longitudinal studies that began with gender-nonconforming boys around age 7 and followed them into adolescence and adulthood; a majority (63%) of the gender nonconforming boys become gay or bisexual as adults.<ref>Zucker, K. J. (1990). "Gender identity disorders in children: Clinical descriptions and natural history". In R. Blanchard & B. W. Steiner (Eds.), ''Clinical management of gender identity disorders in children and adults'' (pp. 1–23). Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Press.</ref>

Two criticisms of Bem's theory in the journal ''[[Psychological Review]]'' concluded that "studies cited by Bem and additional research show that Exotic Becomes Erotic theory is not supported by scientific evidence."<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=Peplau|first1=Letitia A.|last2=Garnets|first2=Linda D.|last3=Spalding|first3=Leah R|last4=Conley|first4=Terri D.|last5=Veniegas|first5=Rosemary C.|date=May 1998|title=A Critique of Bern's "Exotic Becomes Erotic" Theory of Sexual Orientation|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/13707155|format=PDF|journal=Psychological Review|volume=105|issue=2|pages=387–394|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.105.2.387|pmid=9577243|via=PubMed}}</ref> Bem was criticized for relying on a non-random sample of gay men from the 1970s (rather than collecting new data) and for drawing conclusions that appear to contradict the original data. An "examination of the original data showed virtually all respondents were familiar with children of both sexes", and that only 9% of gay men said that "none or only a few" of their friends were male, and most gay men (74%) reported having "an especially close friend of the same sex" during grade school.<ref name=":1" /> Further, "71% of gay men reported feeling different from other boys, but so did 38% of heterosexual men. The difference for gay men is larger, but still indicates that feeling different from same-sex peers was common for heterosexual men." Bem also acknowledged that gay men were more likely to have older brothers (the [[Fraternal birth order and male sexual orientation|fraternal birth order effect]]), which appeared to contradict an unfamiliarity with males. Bem cited cross-cultural studies which also "appear to contradict the EBE theory assertion", such as the [[Sambia people|Sambia]] tribe in Papua New Guinea, which ritually enforced homosexual acts among teenagers, yet once these boys reached adulthood, only a small proportion of men continued to engage in homosexual behaviour - similar to levels observed in the United States.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, Bem's model could be interpreted as implying that if one could change a child's behavior, one could change their sexual orientation, but most psychologists doubt this would be possible.<ref name="Lehmiller">{{Cite book|last=Lehmiller|first=Justin J.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=I3JGDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA156|title=The Psychology of Human Sexuality|date=2017-12-26|publisher=John Wiley & Sons|isbn=978-1-119-16471-5|pages=156–157|language=en}}</ref>

Neuroscientist [[Simon LeVay]] has said that while the theory was arranged in a "believable temporal order",<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=LeVay|first=Simon|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=arJjDQAAQBAJ&q=Again,%20then,%20Bem%E2%80%99s%20%E2%80%9Cexotic%20becomes%20erotic%E2%80%9D%20theory%20lacks%20empirical%20support.&pg=PA65|title=Gay, Straight, and the Reason Why: The Science of Sexual Orientation|date=2016-08-01|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-029738-1|language=en}}</ref>{{rp|65}} that it ultimately "lacks empirical support".<ref name=":0" />{{rp|164}} Social psychologist [[Justin Lehmiller|Justin LehMiller]] stated that "Bem's theory has received a lot of praise for the way it seamlessly links biological and environmental influences" and that there "is also some support for the model in the sense that childhood gender nonconformity is indeed one of the strongest predicators of adult homosexuality", but that the validity of the model "has been questioned on numerous grounds and scientists have largely rejected it."<ref name="Lehmiller"/>

===Ganzfeld experiment===
In [[parapsychology]], Bem is known for his defense of the [[ganzfeld experiment]] as evidence of [[Psi (parapsychology)|psi]], more commonly known as [[extrasensory perception]] or [[psychic]] phenomena.<ref name=BemHonorton1994>{{cite journal |last=Bem |first=Daryl J. |author2=Honorton, Charles |title=Does psi exist? Replicable evidence for an anomalous process of information transfer |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=115 |issue=1 |pages=4–18 |date=January 1994 |url=http://www.dbem.ws/Does%20Psi%20Exist%3F.pdf |access-date=2007-07-31 |doi=10.1037/0033-2909.115.1.4 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170317150522/http://www.dbem.ws/Does%20Psi%20Exist?.pdf |archive-date=2017-03-17 }}</ref>

Bem and [[Charles Honorton]] (1994) reviewed the experimental arrangements of the autoganzfeld experiments, and concluded they provided excellent security against deception by subjects and [[sensory cue]]s.<ref name=BemHonorton1994/> However, [[Ray Hyman]] disagreed with Bem and Honorton as he claims to have discovered some interesting patterns in the data that implied visual cues may have taken place in the experiments. Hyman wrote that the autoganzfeld experiments were flawed because they did not preclude the possibility of [[sensory leakage]].<ref name="Hyman2007">[[Ray Hyman]]. ''Evaluating Parapsychological Claims''. In Robert J. Sternberg, Henry L. Roediger, Diane F. Halpern. (2007). ''Critical Thinking in Psychology''. Cambridge University Press. pp. 216-231.</ref> Bem and Honorton's review was criticized by the scientific community as it contained errors.<ref name="Hyman2007"/><ref name="Wiseman1999">{{cite journal | title=Does Psi Exist? Lack of Replication of an Anomalous Process of Information Transfer | author=Milton, Wiseman|journal=Psychological Bulletin | year=1999 | volume= 125 | issue= 4 | pages=387–391 | doi=10.1037/0033-2909.125.4.387 | pmid=10414223 | last2=Wiseman | first2=R.}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lilenfeld | first1 = Scott | year = 1999 | title = New Analyses Raise Doubts About Replicability of ESP Findings | url = http://www.csicop.org/si/show/new_analyses_raise_doubts_about_replicability_of_esp_findings/ | journal = [[Skeptical Inquirer]] | volume = 23 | pages = 9–12 }}</ref><ref>[[Terence Hines]]. (2003). ''Pseudoscience and the Paranormal''. Prometheus Books. p. 138. {{ISBN|1-57392-979-4}}</ref> Julie Milton and [[Richard Wiseman]] (1999) who discovered errors in Bem's research carried out a meta-analysis of ganzfeld experiments in other laboratories. They found no psi effect, the results showed no effect greater than chance from a database of 30 experiments and a non-[[Statistical significance|significant]] [[Z-test|Stouffer Z]] of 0.70.<ref name="Wiseman1999"/>

Psychologist [[Susan Blackmore]] also criticized Bem's review of the Ganzfeld literature, noting that of the nine studies that were used for the review, five came from one laboratory (Chuck Honorton's). Blackmore also noted that Bem included experiments from [[Carl Sargent]] in the review, and Blackmore had previously found that Sargent had "deliberately violated his own protocols and in one trial had almost certainly cheated." According to Blackmore, psychologists reading Bem's review in ''Psychological Bulletin'' would "not have a clue that serious doubt had been cast on more than a quarter of the studies involved". Blackmore recounts having a discussion with Bem at a consciousness conference where she challenged him on his support of Sargent and Honorton's research; he replied "it did not matter". Writing for ''[[Skeptical Inquirer]]'' Blackmore states "But it does matter. ... It matters because Bem's continued claims mislead a willing public into believing that there is reputable scientific evidence for ESP in the Ganzfeld when there is not".<ref name="BlackmoreSI2018">{{cite journal |last1=Blackmore |first1=Susan |author-link= Susan Blackmore|title=Daryl Bem and Psi in the Ganzfeld |journal=Skeptical Inquirer |date=2018 |volume=42 |issue=3 |pages=44–45 |publisher=Committee for Skeptical Inquiry}}</ref><ref name="BlackmoreSI2019" />

=== "Feeling the Future" controversy ===
In 2011, Bem published the article "Feeling the Future: Experimental Evidence for Anomalous Retroactive Influences on Cognition and Affect" in the ''[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]'' that offered statistical evidence for [[Psi (parapsychology)|psi]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Bem|first=D. J.|title=Feeling the future: experimental evidence for anomalous retroactive influences on cognition and affect.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=March 2011|volume=100|issue=3|pages=407–25|doi=10.1037/a0021524|pmid=21280961|s2cid=1961013 |url=http://dbem.ws/FeelingFuture.pdf|access-date=2011-01-11|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120103233037/http://dbem.ws/FeelingFuture.pdf|archive-date=2012-01-03}}</ref> The article's findings challenged modern scientific conceptions about the unidirectional nature of time. Its presentation by a respected researcher, and its publication by an upper-tier journal, engendered much controversy. In addition to criticism of the paper itself,<ref>[[James Alcock]], [http://www.csicop.org/specialarticles/show/back_from_the_future ''Back from the Future: Parapsychology and the Bem Affair''], March/April 2011 [[Skeptical Inquirer]], January 6, 2011.</ref> the paper's publication prompted a wider debate on the validity of peer review process for allowing such a paper to be published.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.nytimes.com/roomfordebate/2011/01/06/the-esp-study-when-science-goes-psychic/when-peer-review-falters | work=The New York Times | title=Room for Debate: When Peer Review Falters | date=January 7, 2011}}</ref> Bem appeared on MSNBC<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.nbcnews.com/id/21134540|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130129090138/http://www.nbcnews.com/id/21134540/|archive-date=January 29, 2013|title=Professor: Strong evidence ESP is real|work=[[NBC News]]|date=2008-01-23|access-date=January 30, 2011}}</ref> and ''[[The Colbert Report]]''<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.colbertnation.com/the-colbert-report-videos/372474/january-27-2011/time-traveling-porn---daryl-bem|title=The Colbert Report: January 27, 2011 — Brian Greene
|publisher=[[Comedy Central]]|date=2008-01-23|access-date=January 30, 2011}}</ref> to discuss the experiment. American author and researcher [[Mitch Horowitz]] has noted that Bem's experiments in precognition and retrocausality, which have been validated in a meta-analysis of 90 trials in 33 labs in 14 nations, comport not only with relativity but also with interpretations of quantum physics.<ref>{{cite news |access-date=October 7, 2024 |url=https://boingboing.net/2022/08/17/is-precognition-real.html |title=Is Precognition Real? |work=Boing Boing |location=San Francisco, CA |date=August 17, 2022 |author=Horowitz, Mitch}}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine | author=Menuez, Bobbi Salvör| title=Interview: Mitch Horowitz | url=https://archive.org/details/mitch-horowitz-purple-mag-interview-lr/mode/2up | magazine=[[Purple (magazine)|Purple]] | location=Paris, France | publisher=Les Editions Purple | date=September 1, 2024 |issue=#42 | page=219 | access-date=October 7, 2024}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Feeling the future: A meta-analysis of 90 experiments on the anomalous anticipation of random future events |work=National Institutes of Health (NIH) |date=January 29, 2016|pmc=4706048 |last1=Bem |first1=D. |last2=Tressoldi |first2=P. |last3=Rabeyron |first3=T. |last4=Duggan |first4=M. |volume=4 |page=1188 |pmid=26834996 }}</ref>

Wagenmakers ''et al.'' criticized Bem's statistical methodology, saying that he incorrectly provides one-sided [[p-value|''p''-value]] when he should have used a two-sided ''p''-value.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Wagenmakers|first=E. J. |author2=Wetzels, R. |author3=Borsboom, D. |author4=van der Maas, H. L.|title=Why psychologists must change the way they analyze their data: the case of psi: comment on Bem (2011).|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=March 2011|volume=100|issue=3|pages=426–32|doi=10.1037/a0022790|pmid=21280965|url=http://www.ejwagenmakers.com/2011/WagenmakersEtAl2011_JPSP.pdf}}</ref> This could account for the marginally-significant results of his experiment. Bem and two statisticians subsequently published a rebuttal to this critique in the ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology.''<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Bem, D. J. |author2=Utts, J. |author3=Johnson, W. O. |title=Must psychologists change the way they analyze their data?|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=October 2011|volume=101|issue=4|pages=716–719 |doi=10.1037/a0024777 |url=https://dl.dropbox.com/u/8290411/ResponsetoWagenmakers.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110714111153/https://dl.dropbox.com/u/8290411/ResponsetoWagenmakers.pdf|archive-date=2011-07-14|pmid=21928916|citeseerx=10.1.1.694.2507 }}</ref>

Jeffrey Rouder and Richard Morey, who applied a meta-analytical [[Bayes factor]] to Bem's data, concluded, "We remain unconvinced of the viability of ESP. There is no plausible mechanism for it, and it seems contradicted by well-substantiated theories in both physics and biology. Against this background, a change in odds of 40 is negligible.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Rouder | first1 = J. | last2 = Morey | first2 = R. | year = 2011 | title = A Bayes factor meta-analysis of Bem's ESP claim | journal = Psychonomic Bulletin & Review | volume = 18 | issue = 4| pages = 682–689 | doi=10.3758/s13423-011-0088-7| pmid = 21573926 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>[https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/05/110518080059.htm "Odds are against ESP: New statistical approach doesn't support claims that extra-sensory perception exists"]. [[Science Daily]].</ref>

After evaluating Bem's nine experiments, psychologist [[James Alcock]] said that he found metaphorical "dirty test tubes," or serious methodological flaws, such as changing the procedures partway through the experiments and combining results of tests with different chances of significance. It is unknown how many tests were actually performed, nor is there an explanation of how it was determined that participants had "settled down" after seeing erotic images. Alcock concludes that "Just about everything that could be done wrong in an experiment occurred here".<ref>[[James Alcock]], [http://www.csicop.org/specialarticles/show/back_from_the_future ''Back from the Future: Parapsychology and the Bem Affair''], March/April 2011 [[Skeptical Inquirer]], January 6, 2011.</ref> Bem's response to Alcock's critique appeared online at the ''Skeptical Inquirer'' website,<ref>{{cite web|last=Bem|first=Daryl|title=Response to Alcock's "Back from the Future: Comments on Bem"|url=https://skepticalinquirer.org/exclusive/response-to-alcocks-back-from-the-future-comments-on-bem/|date=January 6, 2011|access-date=January 31, 2012}}</ref> and Alcock replied to these comments in a third article at the same website.<ref>{{cite web|last=Alcock|first=James|title=Response to Bem's Comments|url=https://skepticalinquirer.org/exclusive/response-to-bems-comments/|date=January 6, 2011|access-date=January 31, 2012}}</ref>

One of the nine experiments in Bem's study ("Retroactive Facilitation of Recall") was repeated by scientists [[Stuart J. Ritchie]], [[Chris French]], and [[Richard Wiseman]]. Their attempt to replicate was published in ''[[PLoS ONE]]'' and found no evidence of precognition.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Ritchie|first=Stuart J. |author2=Wiseman, Richard |author3=French, Christopher C. |author4=Gilbert, Sam|title=Failing the Future: Three Unsuccessful Attempts to Replicate Bem's 'Retroactive Facilitation of Recall' Effect|journal=PLOS ONE|date=March 2012|volume=7|issue=3|article-number=e33423|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0033423|pmid=22432019|pmc=3303812|bibcode=2012PLoSO...733423R |editor1-last=Gilbert|editor1-first=Sam|doi-access=free }}</ref> Several failed attempts by the authors to publish their replication attempt highlighted difficulties in publishing replications, attracting media attention over concerns of publication bias.<ref>{{cite news|last=Goldacre|first=Ben|title=Backwards step on looking into the future|url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2011/apr/23/ben-goldacre-bad-science|access-date=March 17, 2012|newspaper=[[The Guardian]]|date=April 23, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Aldhous|first=Peter|title=Journal rejects studies contradicting precognition|url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/dn20447-journal-rejects-studies-contradicting-precognition.html|access-date=March 17, 2012|newspaper=[[New Scientist]]|date=May 5, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Jarrett|first=Christian|title=Psi study highlights replication problems|url=http://www.thepsychologist.org.uk/blog/blogpost.cfm?threadid=1984&catid=48|access-date=March 17, 2012|newspaper=[[British Psychological Society|The Psychologist]]|date=May 11, 2011|archive-date=December 28, 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111228221932/http://www.thepsychologist.org.uk/blog/blogpost.cfm?threadid=1984&catid=48}}</ref>  The ''Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'', ''[[Science (journal)|Science Brevia]]'' and ''[[Psychological Science (journal)|Psychological Science]]'' each rejected the paper on the grounds that it was a replication.<ref name="French12"/> A fourth journal, the ''[[British Journal of Psychology]]'', refused the paper after reservations from one referee, later confirmed to be Bem himself, who "might possibly have a conflict of interest with respect to [the] ... submission."<ref name=French12>{{cite news|last=French |author-link=Chris French|first=Chris|title=Precognition studies and the curse of the failed replications|url=https://www.theguardian.com/science/2012/mar/15/precognition-studies-curse-failed-replications|access-date=March 17, 2012|newspaper=[[The Guardian]]|date=March 15, 2012}}</ref> Wiseman set up a register to keep track of other replicating efforts to avoid problems with publication bias, and planned to conduct a meta-analysis on registered replication efforts.<ref>{{cite news|last=Goldacre|first=Ben|title=Backwards step on looking into the future|url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2011/apr/23/ben-goldacre-bad-science|access-date=February 1, 2012|newspaper=The Guardian|date=April 23, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last=Wiseman|first=Richard|title=Replication Registry: Are you conducting a replication of Daryl Bem's 'Feeling the Future' experiments?|url=http://www.richardwiseman.com/BemReplications.shtml|access-date=February 1, 2012}}</ref>

In 2012, two independent articles found that the number of rejections of the [[null hypothesis]] reported by Bem (nine out of ten tested) is abnormally high, given the properties of the experiments and reported effect sizes (Francis, 2012; Schimmack, 2012<ref>Schimmack, U. (2012). The Ironic Effect of Significant Results on the Credibility of Multiple-Study Articles. Psychological Methods, 17, 551–566</ref>). Schimmack (2015)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://replicationindex.wordpress.com/2014/12/30/the-test-of-insufficient-variance-tiva-a-new-tool-for-the-detection-of-questionable-research-practices/ |title=The Test of Insufficient Variance (TIVA): A New Tool for the Detection of Questionable Research Practices &#124; Replicability-Index |publisher=Replicationindex.wordpress.com |date=2014-12-30 |access-date=2018-12-11}}</ref> used a more powerful test to reveal selection for significance, the Test of Insufficient Variance, and found even stronger evidence that the reported studies are biased in favor of supporting ESP. These findings imply that studies with non-significant results are missing and the reported evidence overstates the strength of the effect and evidence. According to Francis, this suggests that Bem's experiments cannot be taken as a proper scientific study, as critical data is likely unavailable.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Francis|first=G.|title=Too good to be true: Publication bias in two prominent studies from experimental psychology|journal=Psychonomic Bulletin & Review|date=February 2012|volume=19|issue=2|pages=151–156|doi=10.3758/s13423-012-0227-9|pmid=22351589|s2cid=14700726|url=http://www2.psych.purdue.edu/~gfrancis/Publications/GFrancis-R1.pdf}}</ref>

The publication of Bem's article and the resulting controversy prompted a wide-ranging commentary by Etienne LeBel and Kurt Peters.<ref>{{cite journal|last=LeBel|first=Etienne P.|author2=Peters, Kurt R.|title=Fearing the future of empirical psychology: Bem's (2011) evidence of psi as a case study of deficiencies in modal research practice|journal=Review of General Psychology|date=January 2011|volume=15|issue=4|pages=371–379|doi=10.1037/a0025172|s2cid=51686730|url=http://psychology.okstate.edu/faculty/jgrice/psyc5314/ModalResearchProg_2011.pdf}}</ref> Using Bem's article as a case study, they discussed deficiencies in the accepted methodology most commonly used in experimental psychology. LeBel and Peters suggest that experimental psychology is systemically biased toward interpretations of data that favor the researcher's theory.

In 2012, the same journal that published Bem's original experiments, ''The Journal of Personality and Social Psychology'' (Vol. 103, No. 6), published "Correcting the Past: Failures to Replicate Psi" by Jeff Galak of [[Carnegie Mellon University]], Robyn A. LeBoeuf of the [[University of Florida]], Leif D. Nelson of the [[University of California at Berkeley]], and Joseph P. Simmons of the [[University of Pennsylvania]]. The paper reported seven experiments testing for precognition that "found no evidence supporting its existence."<ref>{{cite web|last=Frazier|first=Kendrick|title=Failure to Replicate Results of Bem Parapsychology Experiments Published by Same Journal|url=http://www.csicop.org/si/show/failure_to_replicate_results_of_bem_parapsychology_experiments_published_by|publisher=csicop.org|access-date=August 7, 2013|archive-date=December 23, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171223080039/https://www.csicop.org/si/show/failure_to_replicate_results_of_bem_parapsychology_experiments_published_by}}</ref>

In a 2017 follow-up article in ''Slate'' magazine on the "Feeling the Future" experiments, Bem is quoted as saying, "I'm all for rigor, but I prefer other people do it. I see its importance—it's fun for some people—but I don't have the patience for it."  The article continues: "It's been hard for him, he said, to move into a field where the data count for so much. "If you looked at all my past experiments, they were always rhetorical devices. I gathered data to show how my point would be made. I used data as a point of persuasion, and I never really worried about, 'Will this replicate or will this not?'""<ref name=Slate052017>{{cite news |title=Daryl Bem Proved ESP Is Real: Which means science is broken. |url=https://redux.slate.com/cover-stories/2017/05/daryl-bem-proved-esp-is-real-showed-science-is-broken.html |date=May 17, 2017 |author=Engber, Daniel |work=Slate}}</ref> While fellow psychologist [[Stuart Vyse]] sees this statement as coming "remarkably close to an outright admission of [[Data dredging|p-hacking]]", he also notes that Bem "has been given substantial credit for stimulating the movement to tighten the standards for research" such as that taking place in [[open science]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vyse |first1=Stuart |author-link=Stuart Vyse|title=P-Hacking Confessions: Daryl Bem and Me |journal=[[Skeptical Inquirer]] |date=2017 |volume=41 |issue=5 |pages=25–27 |url=https://www.csicop.org/specialarticles/show/p-hacker_confessions_daryl_bem_and_me |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180805142806/https://www.csicop.org/specialarticles/show/p-hacker_confessions_daryl_bem_and_me |archive-date=2018-08-05 |access-date=August 5, 2018}}</ref>

A large-scale pre-registered replication in 2023, which included Bem and other ESP proponents in its design, found no evidence for precognition.<ref>[https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/full/10.1098/rsos.191375 "Raising the value of research studies in psychological science by increasing the credibility of research reports: the transparent Psi project"], Kekecs et al 2023 (''R. Soc. open'' DOI:sci.10191375191375)</ref>

== Family life ==

Bem married [[Sandra Bem]] (née Lipsitz),<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Nussbaum |first1=Emily |date=May–June 1998 |title=Does the exotic become erotic? |journal=Lingua Franca |url=http://www.emilynussbaum.com/lingua_franca/1998/05/does_the_exotic_become_erotic.php |access-date=January 8, 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110710190734/http://www.emilynussbaum.com/lingua_franca/1998/05/does_the_exotic_become_erotic.php |archive-date=July 10, 2011 }}</ref> also a psychology professor, in 1965. Though still legally married, they were "amicably separated" from 1994 until her death in 2014.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.news.cornell.edu/stories/2014/05/sandra-bem-feminist-scholar-and-psychologist-dies |title=and S. Bem, An Unconventional Family (1998) |publisher=News.cornell.edu |date=2014-05-22 |access-date=2018-12-11}}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine|last1=Henig|first1=Robin|title=The Last Day of Her Life|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2015/05/17/magazine/the-last-day-of-her-life.html|magazine=The New York Times Magazine|access-date=May 16, 2015|date=May 14, 2015}}</ref> In 2015, he married his partner of twenty years, Ithaca College professor of communication studies,  performance studies, and queer studies Bruce Henderson. They live in Ithaca, NY.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Kois |first1=Dan |title=The Case for ESP -- New York Magazine - Nymag |url=https://nymag.com/news/features/bem-esp-2011-3/ |access-date=14 November 2020 |work=New York Magazine |date=25 February 2011 |language=en-us}}</ref><!-- Per citation celebrated 15th anniversary in February 2011. -->

==Selected publications==
{{refbegin}}
* Bem, D. J. (1972).  Self-Perception Theory. In L. Berkowitz (Ed.), ''Advances in Experimental Social Psychology''  (Vol. 6, pp.&nbsp;1–62). New York: Academic Press, {{ISBN|978-0-12-015206-3}}
* Bem, D. J. (1970). ''Beliefs, Attitudes, and Human Affairs'' - Wadsworth Pub Co, {{ISBN|978-0-8185-8906-5}}
* R. L. Atkinson, R. C. Atkinson, E. E. Smith, D. J. Bem.  Introduction to Psychology 1990 - Harcourt Brace Jovanovich
* Bem, D. J. An experimental analysis of self-persuasion. Attitude change: the competing views, 1971
* A. Caspi, G. H. Elder, D. J. Bem.  Moving against the world: Life-course patterns of explosive children. Developmental Psychology, 1987
* A. Caspi, G. H. Elder, D. J. Bem.  Moving away from the world: Life-course patterns of shy children. Developmental Psychology, 1988
* D. J. Bem, C. Honorton.  Does psi exist? Replicable evidence for an anomalous process of information transfer. Psychological Bulletin, 1994
* D. J. Bem, H. K. McConnell.  Testing the self-perception explanation of dissonance phenomena: On the salience of premanipulation attitudes. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 1970
* {{cite journal | pmid = 11100261 | volume=29 | issue=6 | title=Exotic becomes erotic: interpreting the biological correlates of sexual orientation |date=December 2000 | journal=Arch Sex Behav | pages=531–48 | doi = 10.1023/A:1002050303320 | last1 = Bem | first1 = D. J.| s2cid=12078658 }}
* {{cite journal | pmid = 9577244 | volume=105 | issue=2 | title=Is EBE theory supported by the evidence? Is it androcentric? A reply to Peplau et al. (1998) |date=April 1998 | journal=Psychol Rev | pages=395–8 | doi = 10.1037/0033-295X.105.2.395 | last1 = Bem | first1 = D. J.}}
* {{cite journal | pmid = 2769561 | volume=57 | issue=2 | title=Continuities and consequences of interactional styles across the life course |date=June 1989 | journal=J Pers | pages=375–406 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-6494.1989.tb00487.x | last1 = Caspi | first1 = A | last2 = Bem | first2 = D. J. | last3 = Elder Jr | first3 = G. H.}}
* S. L. Bem, D. J. Bem  Does Sex-biased Job Advertising" Aid and Abet" Sex Discrimination? Journal of Applied Social Psychology, 1973
* {{cite journal | pmid = 6843717 | title=Toward a response style theory of persons in situations | year=1983 | journal=Nebr Symp Motiv | pages=201–31 | last1 = Bem | first1 = D. J.}}
* {{cite journal | pmid = 894467 | volume=45 | issue=3 | title=Predicting more of the people more of the time: some thoughts on the Allen-Potkay studies of intraindividual variability |date=September 1977 | journal=J Pers | pages=327–33 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-6494.1977.tb00155.x | last1 = Bem | first1 = D. J.}}
* {{cite journal | pmid = 5013145 | volume=40 | issue=1 | title=Constructing cross-situational consistencies in behavior: some thoughts on Alker's critique of Mischel |date=March 1972 | journal=J Pers | pages=17–26 | doi = 10.1111/j.1467-6494.1972.tb00645.x | last1 = Bem | first1 = D. J.}}
* {{cite journal | pmid = 5435536 | volume=14 | issue=1 | title=Testing the self-perception explanation of dissonance phenomena: on the salience of premanipulation attitudes |date=January 1970 | journal=J Pers Soc Psychol | pages=23–31 | doi = 10.1037/h0020916 | last1 = Bem | first1 = D. J. | last2 = McConnell | first2 = H. K.| s2cid=26730816 }}
* {{cite journal | pmid = 5342882 | volume=74 | issue=3 | title=Self-perception: An alternative interpretation of cognitive dissonance phenomena |date=May 1967 | journal=Psychol Rev | pages=183–200 | doi = 10.1037/h0024835 | last1 = Bem | first1 = D. J.| citeseerx=10.1.1.323.833 }}
* {{cite journal | pmid = 5939010 | volume=3 | issue=6 | title=Inducing belief in false confessions |date=June 1966 | journal=J Pers Soc Psychol | pages=707–10 | doi = 10.1037/h0023226 | last1 = Bem | first1 = D. J.}}
* {{cite journal | pmid = 14004673 | volume=65 | title=Group influence on individual risk taking |date=August 1962 | journal=J Abnorm Soc Psychol | pages=75–86 | doi = 10.1037/h0044376 | last1 = Wallach | first1 = M. A. | last2 = Kogan | first2 = N. | last3 = Bem | first3 = D. J. | issue = 2| hdl=2027.42/108371 | url = https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/2027.42/108371/1/ets200112.pdf | hdl-access = free }}
{{refend}}
